# From Bearden to the Present: The Rise and Persistence of Private Probation in Georgia {#sec-appendix-private-probation}

```{=latex}
\setcounter{page}{1}
\renewcommand{\thepage}{D-\arabic{page}}
```

## Introduction: The Constitutional Promise (1983)

In 1983, the Supreme Court decided *Bearden v. Georgia*, a case that appeared to close the door on a practice as old as the republic: imprisoning people for their poverty. Danny Bearden had been convicted of burglary and theft in a Georgia state court. The trial court sentenced him to three years on probation, with the first year to be served in a "boot camp" program and the remainder on regular probation. As conditions of probation, Bearden was required to pay a $500 fine and $250 in restitution. He was given four months to pay.

Bearden made an initial payment of $200, but then lost his job. He was laid off from his employment and, despite repeated efforts, could not find new work. Unable to make further payments, he notified his probation officer that he would not be able to pay the remaining balance. The probation officer filed a petition to revoke Bearden's probation. At the revocation hearing, Bearden testified about his efforts to find work and his inability to pay. The trial court acknowledged that Bearden had tried to find employment but concluded that because he had failed to make the required payments, revocation was mandatory. The court sentenced him to serve the remaining portion of his probation period in prison.

The question before the Supreme Court was whether the Constitution permitted this result—whether a court could imprison a probationer solely for inability to pay, when the probationer had made bona fide efforts to find the money but simply could not do so.

The Court held unanimously that it could not. Writing for the Court, Justice O'Connor explained that imprisoning a person solely because of inability to pay a fine or restitution violates the Equal Protection Clause of the Fourteenth Amendment. The state may not "impose a fine as a sentence and then automatically convert it into a jail term solely because the defendant is indigent and cannot forthwith pay the fine in full." Such a practice would punish defendants not for what they did but for who they are—for the circumstance of poverty rather than for any culpable conduct.

The Court established a two-part framework for probation revocation based on nonpayment. First, the sentencing court must inquire into the reasons for the failure to pay. If the defendant made sufficient bona fide efforts to acquire the resources to pay, and if alternative measures of punishment are adequate to meet the state's interests in punishment and deterrence, then imprisonment is inappropriate. Second, only if the defendant's failure to pay was "willful"—if the defendant had the resources to pay but chose not to, or if the defendant failed to make sufficient efforts to acquire resources—may the court imprison for nonpayment.

*Bearden* was the capstone of what scholars have called the Supreme Court's "debtors' prison trilogy." The doctrinal foundation had been laid in two earlier decisions that addressed different procedural contexts but established the same constitutional principle.

In *Williams v. Illinois* (1970), the Court addressed what happened when a defendant completed a jail sentence but still owed a fine. Willie Williams had been sentenced to one year in jail, the statutory maximum for his offense, plus a $500 fine. Illinois law provided that defendants who could not pay their fines would "work off" the debt at the rate of $5 per day in additional jail time. This meant that Williams, who was indigent, would serve an additional 100 days beyond his one-year sentence—time that no non-indigent defendant would serve. The Court held that this violated equal protection: "the Equal Protection Clause of the Fourteenth Amendment requires that the statutory ceiling placed on imprisonment for any substantive offense be the same for all defendants irrespective of their economic status."

In *Tate v. Short* (1971), the Court extended the principle to defendants whose only sentence was a fine. Preston Tate accumulated $425 in traffic fines. Texas law provided that defendants who could not pay would be imprisoned at the rate of $5 per day. Because Tate was indigent, he faced 85 days in jail—imprisonment that would not be imposed on any defendant who could pay. The Court held that "the Constitution prohibits the State from imposing a fine as a sentence and then automatically converting it into a jail term solely because the defendant is indigent and cannot forthwith pay the fine in full."

*Bearden* completed the doctrinal structure. *Williams* addressed the extension of a jail sentence beyond the maximum. *Tate* addressed the conversion of a fine-only sentence into jail time. *Bearden* addressed the revocation of probation for failure to pay. Together, the three cases established that at every stage of the criminal process—initial sentencing, completion of incarceration, and probation supervision—the Constitution prohibits punishing poverty itself.

The doctrinal logic was rooted in equal protection, but it drew as well on fundamental fairness and due process concerns. Wealthy defendants could pay their fines and walk free; poor defendants could not. This differential treatment based solely on financial status created an unconstitutional disparity that bore no relationship to culpability or desert. The state's interest in collecting fines, while legitimate, could not justify imprisoning people for what amounted to their economic condition—a status over which they had limited control. The Constitution required alternatives for those who could not pay: community service, extended payment plans, or, ultimately, forgiveness of the debt.

*Bearden* appeared to establish a clear and administrable principle. Courts across the country cited it as closing the door on debtors' prisons—the practice, common in eighteenth and nineteenth century America and England, of imprisoning people solely for unpaid debts. The decision seemed to guarantee that no one would be jailed simply for being too poor to pay. For many observers, the debtors' prison was a relic of history, abolished by the Supreme Court's trilogy of decisions.

What followed in Georgia over the next four decades demonstrated how constitutional protections can be circumvented through institutional design, categorical distinctions, and the privatization of governmental functions.

## The Legislature Opens the Door (1991)

Eight years after *Bearden*, the Georgia General Assembly enacted legislation that would transform misdemeanor justice in the state. The 1991 statute authorized local governments to contract with private companies to provide probation supervision services. The companies would supervise probationers, collect payments, and enforce compliance—all at no cost to the government. The arrangement was called "offender-funded" probation: the probationers themselves would pay for their supervision through monthly fees.

The innovation solved a problem that local governments across Georgia faced. Misdemeanor courts processed enormous numbers of cases—traffic offenses, minor thefts, disorderly conduct, possession of small amounts of marijuana. Many defendants could not pay their fines immediately. Courts had two options: allow defendants to leave without paying, which meant the fines often went uncollected, or incarcerate defendants until they could pay, which was both costly (jails are expensive to operate) and, after *Bearden*, constitutionally suspect. Probation offered a middle path: defendants could be released and given time to pay, with the threat of revocation if they failed to comply.

But probation supervision was itself expensive. Probation officers had to be hired, trained, and paid. Offices had to be maintained. Records had to be kept. Local governments faced budget constraints that made expanding public probation difficult. The private probation model offered an apparent solution: companies would provide all of these services, and the government would not have to pay a cent. The probationers would cover the costs.

The legal architecture of private probation was designed to avoid the constitutional constraints that *Bearden* had established. The key move was categorical: the fees that private probation companies charged were not "fines"—they were "supervision costs." Fines were punishment for the offense; supervision costs were the administrative expense of monitoring compliance. This distinction mattered because *Bearden* addressed what happens when a defendant cannot pay a fine. It did not directly address what happens when a defendant cannot pay the costs of supervision.

The distinction was formalistic, but it had real legal consequences. A fine is a penalty imposed as punishment for criminal conduct. A supervision fee, in the framing that private probation companies and their government partners adopted, is something different: a fee for services rendered, like a utility bill or a rental payment. The defendant receives a service (supervision) and pays for that service. If the defendant does not pay, the defendant is not being punished for poverty—the defendant is being held accountable for failing to pay for services received.

This framing allowed private probation to operate in the space that *Bearden* appeared to have closed. Courts could place defendants on probation, assign them to private companies, and threaten them with incarceration for nonpayment of fees—all without technically violating the principle that poverty cannot be criminalized. The fees were not fines. The revocation was not for the original offense. The system operated around the constitutional protection rather than through it.

Georgia's withdrawal from misdemeanor supervision completed the transformation. As private companies expanded, public probation offices reduced their involvement in misdemeanor cases. By the 2000s, most Georgia courts had no public probation option for misdemeanors. If a defendant could not pay a fine immediately and wanted to avoid jail, private probation was the only choice. The market that the 1991 legislation created had become, for practical purposes, mandatory.

## The Industry Takes Root (1992–2010)

The first private probation contract in Georgia was signed in 1992, when Chatham County (home to Savannah) contracted with Professional Corrections Services. The company agreed to supervise misdemeanor probationers, collect court-ordered fines and restitution, and charge probationers monthly supervision fees for its services. The arrangement was framed as a win-win: the county would receive the fines and restitution it was owed, without having to spend resources on collection or supervision; the company would keep the supervision fees as its revenue, creating a profitable business; and the probationers would receive supervision services and the opportunity to pay their debts over time rather than immediately. The costs fell on the probationers, but they were characterized as reasonable fees for valuable services.

Other companies quickly entered the market. Professional Probation Services (PPS) was founded in 1992 and became one of the largest operators in the state, eventually expanding to Alabama, Mississippi, and Tennessee. Georgia Probation Services launched in 1993, focusing on suburban Atlanta counties. Sentinel Offender Services, a California-based company that had been founded in 1990, expanded into Georgia in 1993 and grew aggressively, eventually becoming the largest for-profit probation company in the country with operations in multiple states. CSRA Probation Services was founded in 1997 in Augusta, taking its name from the Central Savannah River Area, and grew to dominate the eastern part of the state. Judicial Correction Services (JCS) was founded in 2001, and Southeast Corrections, founded in 2005, added to the competition.

The expansion was rapid. By 2000, private probation companies operated in dozens of Georgia courts. The growth was driven by local demand: courts faced crowded dockets and uncollected fines, and private probation offered a solution that appeared to cost the government nothing. County commissioners and municipal court judges embraced the model. By 2012, according to Human Rights Watch, 648 courts in Georgia used private probation services—far more than any other state. Georgia had become the epicenter of the private probation industry.

The scale of probation supervision in Georgia was extraordinary by national standards. The state had the highest rate of probation supervision in the nation: approximately one in every 25 Georgia adults was on some form of probation, compared to a national average of roughly one in 55. The majority of misdemeanor probationers—those convicted of traffic offenses, minor thefts, marijuana possession, and similar offenses—were supervised by private companies rather than public probation officers.

The business model was straightforward in concept but complex in practice. When a defendant appeared in court for a misdemeanor offense and could not pay the fine immediately, the court would place the defendant on probation. This was often presented as a choice: pay the fine now and leave, or accept probation and pay over time. But for defendants without the resources to pay immediately, probation was the only option other than incarceration. The defendant would be assigned to whichever private probation company had the contract with that court—the defendant had no choice of provider—and required to report monthly.

At each monthly visit, the defendant would pay whatever portion of the fine he or she could afford, plus a supervision fee to the company. The supervision fees typically ranged from $35 to $50 per month, though the exact amount varied by company and jurisdiction. Additional charges could add substantially to the monthly bill: $20 to $30 for each drug test, which some companies required monthly or more frequently; fees for electronic monitoring if imposed; fees for anger management classes, substance abuse treatment, or other programs; and one-time charges for enrollment, late payment, or other administrative matters. A defendant who was required to take monthly drug tests might owe $75 or more per month, in addition to payments toward the underlying fine.

The critical feature of the model was what happened when defendants could not pay. If a defendant missed a payment or failed to appear for a scheduled meeting, the probation company could report the violation to the court. The company itself could not arrest anyone or issue a warrant, but it could—and routinely did—request that the court issue a warrant for the defendant's arrest. Courts generally granted these requests, sometimes with minimal or no independent inquiry into the circumstances.

The defendant would be jailed on the warrant until a hearing could be held. Depending on the court's schedule and the defendant's ability to make bail, this might mean hours, days, or weeks of incarceration. The defendant would then be brought before a judge to explain the failure to comply with probation conditions.

At that hearing, the judge had several options. The judge could extend the probation term, giving the defendant more time to pay. The judge could impose additional conditions, such as requiring more frequent reporting or drug testing. The judge could revoke probation entirely and sentence the defendant to jail for the remainder of the original term. Or, in some cases, the judge might terminate probation entirely if the defendant had made sufficient payments.

The companies had a financial interest in extension rather than termination: a terminated probationer stops paying fees, while an extended probationer continues paying. A revoked probationer also stops paying, but revocation at least demonstrated to other probationers that failure to pay had consequences. The incentives generally pushed toward longer and longer terms of supervision, with fees accumulating at each step.

Probation that was initially imposed for 12 months could stretch to two years, three years, or longer through repeated extensions. Defendants who had originally owed a few hundred dollars in fines could find themselves owing thousands in accumulated supervision fees. The fees were not applied to the underlying fine; they were additional charges on top of what the defendant already owed. A defendant who paid $50 per month for two years would have paid $1,200 to the probation company, plus whatever fraction of the original fine the monthly payments had covered. In many cases, the monthly payments went first to the company's fees, with only the remainder applied to the fine. This meant that the debt to the court decreased slowly if at all, while the total amount extracted from the defendant multiplied.

## The Human Cost: Documentation of Abuses (2010–2014)

The human cost of this system emerged through individual stories that advocacy organizations and journalists began documenting in the early 2010s. The cases followed a pattern that became depressingly familiar.

Thomas Barrett, a homeless Army veteran in Augusta, was convicted of stealing a $2 can of beer from a convenience store. The offense was about as minor as theft could be. Barrett was fined $200. Unable to pay immediately—he was homeless and unemployed—he was placed on private probation with CSRA Probation Services. He was required to report monthly and pay both the fine and monthly supervision fees. When he failed to make payments, his probation was extended. Fees accumulated: each month of nonpayment added another month of supervision and another supervision fee. After months of inability to pay, Barrett was arrested on a warrant and jailed. By the time of his incarceration, the total he owed had grown to over $1,000. His debt had increased fivefold, his freedom had been taken, and he was no closer to paying the original $200 fine than he had been at the beginning.

Gina Ray, a grandmother in Harpersville, Alabama, received a speeding ticket in 2012. She was fined $179. Unable to pay immediately, she was placed on probation with Judicial Correction Services, which also operated in Georgia. The company charged her a $40 monthly supervision fee. Ray made payments when she could, but her income was limited; she was caring for grandchildren while working a low-wage job. When she fell behind, the company added late fees. When she missed a payment entirely, the company reported the violation. She was arrested on a warrant and jailed. Over the course of her time on probation, she paid more than $2,000—for a $179 speeding ticket.

Harriet Cleveland was convicted of shoplifting in Georgia and fined $705. She was placed on probation with Sentinel Offender Services. Cleveland was homeless and disabled; she lived on disability payments that were barely enough to cover basic necessities. Sentinel required her to pay supervision fees in addition to the fine. When she could not pay, the company extended her probation. When she still could not pay, it requested a warrant for her arrest. Cleveland spent more than a year on probation and was arrested multiple times, all for inability to pay a fine that she never had the resources to pay in the first place.

The pattern was consistent across cases: minor offense, inability to pay, assignment to private probation, accumulation of fees, failure to pay, arrest, incarceration. A defendant convicted of a traffic violation might end up spending weeks in jail. A defendant who stole merchandise worth a few dollars might accumulate debts in the thousands. The system produced outcomes that bore no relationship to the severity of the original offense or to any reasonable concept of proportional punishment.

The structural problems went beyond individual cases. Private probation companies operated with an inherent conflict of interest: they were responsible, in practice if not in law, for determining whether defendants had the ability to pay, yet they had a financial interest in concluding that defendants could pay. A finding of inability to pay might lead to termination of probation and loss of revenue; a finding of ability to pay kept the defendant on the company's rolls and the fees flowing.

The conflict of interest was embedded in the system's design. Under *Bearden*, courts were required to determine ability to pay before jailing for nonpayment. But courts relied on probation companies to provide information about defendants' compliance and circumstances. Companies had incentives to characterize nonpayment as willful rather than as inability, because that characterization kept the probation active and the fees accumulating. Even when courts made the formal determination, they often did so based on information provided by companies with financial stakes in the outcome.

Probation officers, in some companies, received bonuses based on the revenue they collected. This created additional pressure to maximize payments and minimize findings of indigency. Officers were not neutral administrators of justice; they were, in effect, collection agents with the power to recommend arrest and incarceration for those who did not pay. The threat of jail was a collection tool, employed to squeeze payments from people who often had nothing left to give.

The lack of transparency made the system difficult to scrutinize. Private probation companies were not required to disclose their revenues, their fee structures, or their collection practices. Courts knew what fines and restitution were being collected, but they had limited visibility into the fees that companies charged, the bonuses that officers received, or the total amounts that defendants paid. Academic researchers and journalists who sought to understand the system found that basic information was unavailable or closely guarded. Companies treated their financial data as proprietary business information, not as public records subject to disclosure.

In February 2014, Human Rights Watch published "Profiting from Probation: America's 'Offender-Funded' Probation Industry." The 72-page report, subtitled "Profiting from Probation," focused on Georgia and Alabama, where the private probation industry was most developed, and documented systemic problems that went beyond individual bad actors.

The report found that private probation companies routinely jailed people for failing to pay without conducting meaningful ability-to-pay inquiries as *Bearden* required. Companies extended probation terms to maximize fee collection, keeping defendants on supervision long past the point when the original offense would have been resolved. Defendants were threatened with arrest and incarceration as collection tools—not as responses to willful defiance, but as ways to pressure payment from people who were broke. And the total amounts extracted from defendants—through supervision fees, drug testing charges, enrollment fees, late fees, and other costs—often exceeded the original fines by multiples of three, four, or five.

Human Rights Watch called the system a "de facto debtors' prison" that violated the principles established in *Bearden*. The constitutional protection against jailing for poverty had been formally preserved—courts still had to make ability-to-pay findings—but the protection had been rendered meaningless in practice. The organization recommended that states prohibit jailing probationers for failure to pay without first conducting a meaningful ability-to-pay determination, that courts conduct these determinations themselves rather than delegating them to probation companies, that fee caps be established to prevent minor offenses from ballooning into crushing debts, and that transparency requirements be imposed so the public could see how the system operated.

The ACLU and other advocacy organizations issued their own reports and began representing defendants challenging their treatment. The Southern Center for Human Rights filed lawsuits on behalf of Georgia defendants who had been jailed for inability to pay. National media outlets, including NPR and the New York Times, published investigations documenting the industry's practices.

Academic researchers began systematic studies of monetary sanctions in the criminal justice system. Sociologist Alexes Harris, in her 2016 book *A Pound of Flesh: Monetary Sanctions as Punishment for the Poor*, examined how fines, fees, and restitution created what she called "permanent punishment" for those caught in the system—a cycle of debt, nonpayment, and consequences that followed defendants for years or decades. Sociologist Sarah Shannon's work focused specifically on Georgia, documenting how private probation operated in practice and quantifying its scope. Her research found that the system was not a marginal phenomenon but a core feature of criminal justice in the state, touching hundreds of thousands of lives.

## Litigation and Judicial Response (2010–2015)

Litigation against private probation companies began to accumulate in the early 2010s, driven by public interest organizations, civil rights lawyers, and a small number of private attorneys who recognized that the industry's practices might violate federal constitutional requirements.

The legal theories were straightforward. *Bearden v. Georgia* required courts to determine that nonpayment was willful before jailing probationers for failure to pay. If courts were jailing people without making that determination—or if they were delegating the determination to probation companies with financial interests in the outcome—then constitutional rights were being violated. The Fourteenth Amendment's due process clause required meaningful hearings before deprivations of liberty. The equal protection clause prohibited punishing people for their poverty. And federal statutes, including the Fair Debt Collection Practices Act, might apply to companies that were, in effect, acting as debt collectors with powers that ordinary debt collectors lacked.

In 2010, a federal district court in *McGee v. Sentinel Offender Services* issued an injunction against Sentinel Offender Services, then the largest private probation company in the country. The plaintiff, Kevin McGee, had been jailed multiple times for failure to pay probation fees. He alleged that Sentinel routinely obtained arrest warrants from courts without providing accurate information about probationers' circumstances or ability to pay, and that the company used the threat of incarceration as a collection tool.

The court found that Sentinel's practices violated due process. Defendants were being jailed without meaningful hearings and without judicial determinations that their nonpayment was willful. The company had a standardized process for requesting warrants that gave courts little basis for independent judgment. The court issued an injunction requiring Sentinel to change its practices in the relevant jurisdiction. Perhaps more significantly, the case established that private probation companies themselves—not merely the courts that employed them—could be held liable for constitutional violations. This opened a new avenue for litigation.

A pattern of individual lawsuits against Sentinel and other companies followed. Plaintiffs alleged false imprisonment for being jailed without proper hearings. They alleged due process violations for being jailed without ability-to-pay determinations. They alleged violations of the Fair Debt Collection Practices Act, arguing that probation companies were engaging in abusive debt collection practices that would be illegal if conducted by ordinary creditors. Some plaintiffs alleged state-law claims for fraud, negligent misrepresentation, and intentional infliction of emotional distress.

Some cases settled before trial, with companies paying modest amounts to individual plaintiffs in exchange for confidentiality agreements. Others were litigated more extensively. The litigation imposed costs on companies—legal fees, settlement payments, management attention, reputational damage—and generated publicity about their practices. News stories about the lawsuits brought attention to the industry in ways that advocacy reports alone had not. But the litigation did not fundamentally alter how the system operated. Companies adjusted their procedures, added disclaimers to their paperwork, and continued operating.

In 2014, the Georgia Supreme Court addressed private probation in a case involving the scope of contracts between courts and companies. The central question was whether certain contracts between municipal courts and private probation companies were legally authorized under Georgia law. The court upheld the constitutionality of private probation in general, rejecting the argument that delegation of supervision to private companies was inherently unconstitutional. The state legislature had authorized such contracts; the constitutional objections, while serious, did not prevail.

But the court also imposed limits that mattered. It ruled against the practice of "tolling"—indefinitely suspending the running of probation terms when defendants failed to pay or failed to report. Tolling had allowed some probation terms to extend for years beyond their original duration. A defendant sentenced to 12 months of probation might find, after three years of sporadic payments and missed appointments, that the probation term had barely begun to run because the company had tolled it whenever the defendant fell behind. The Georgia Supreme Court held that this practice was not authorized by the contracts or by law.

The court also held that defendants who had been supervised under contracts that lacked proper legal authorization might be entitled to reimbursement of fees they had paid. This created potential liability for companies and courts that had entered into contracts without following required procedures. The ruling was narrow—it turned on the specific language of particular contracts—but it signaled that courts would scrutinize private probation arrangements and would not simply defer to whatever practices companies and local governments had developed.

These judicial interventions addressed specific abuses but did not dismantle the system. Courts could still place defendants on private probation. Companies could still charge supervision fees. The threat of incarceration for nonpayment remained. What the litigation accomplished was incremental: particular practices were enjoined, particular companies were held liable in particular cases, but the structure persisted.

Class action litigation emerged as a tool for broader reform. Plaintiffs' attorneys, including lawyers at the Southern Center for Human Rights and private firms working on contingency, began filing suits on behalf of classes of defendants who had been subjected to illegal practices. These cases sought not only damages for past harms but also injunctive relief that would change how companies operated going forward. A successful class action could require a company to change its practices across all the courts it served, not merely the court where a particular plaintiff had been harmed.

The class actions raised the stakes for companies. A single plaintiff might recover a few thousand dollars in damages; a class of thousands of plaintiffs might recover millions. The threat of class certification created pressure to settle and created pressure for systemic change. Even unsuccessful class actions generated discovery—depositions, document productions, internal emails—that revealed how companies actually operated and provided ammunition for advocacy campaigns and further litigation.

## Legislative Reform: House Bill 310 (2015)

The political context in 2015 was favorable to criminal justice reform. Across the country, a bipartisan consensus had emerged that the criminal justice system was too large, too expensive, and too punitive. Conservative groups concerned about government spending and overreach found common ground with progressive groups concerned about mass incarceration and racial disparities. Organizations that rarely agreed on anything—the American Civil Liberties Union, the Koch brothers' network, the evangelical Christian organization Prison Fellowship—all called for reform. The movement was sometimes called "Right on Crime" by conservatives and "smart on crime" by progressives, but the policy proposals often overlapped.

Georgia's Republican Governor, Nathan Deal, had made criminal justice reform a signature issue of his administration. In 2012, he signed HB 1176, which created a system of "accountability courts" as alternatives to incarceration and expanded eligibility for drug courts. In 2013, he signed HB 349, which reformed juvenile justice practices. The legislation had bipartisan support and was championed as a model for other states. Deal spoke frequently about the need to reduce incarceration while maintaining public safety, and he convened a Criminal Justice Reform Council to develop further recommendations.

The Human Rights Watch report in early 2014 and the subsequent national attention to private probation put the issue on the reform agenda. The documentation of abuses was difficult to ignore, and it fit the reform narrative that both parties were embracing: here was a system that cost taxpayers nothing but extracted enormous amounts from people who could not afford it, that jailed people for poverty, and that produced outcomes disconnected from any reasonable concept of justice.

House Bill 310, passed by the Georgia General Assembly and signed by Governor Deal on May 7, 2015, represented the most significant legislative reform of private probation since the industry's creation. The bill was a comprehensive criminal justice package that addressed sentencing, parole, and supervision practices across the state. It ran to dozens of pages and touched many aspects of the criminal justice system. Several provisions directly targeted the abuses that had been documented in private probation.

The bill created the Department of Community Supervision, a new state agency that would oversee probation services across Georgia. The department consolidated functions that had previously been scattered across multiple agencies, including the State Board of Pardons and Paroles and various county-level probation offices. This represented a partial reclaiming of governmental responsibility for supervision that had been ceded to private companies over the previous two decades. The department would have regulatory authority over private probation companies, including the power to establish standards and investigate complaints.

The bill established a three-month cap on "pay-only" probation—probation imposed solely for the purpose of collecting fines, fees, and restitution, without any other supervision conditions. Under the new rule, if a probationer's only obligation was to pay money, and if no other supervision was necessary, probation would automatically terminate after three months regardless of whether the full amount had been paid. This addressed one of the most criticized aspects of private probation: the indefinite extension of supervision solely to collect money. A defendant who owed a $500 fine could no longer be kept on probation for years, accumulating fees, simply because the fine remained unpaid. After three months, the probation would end and the fine would become a civil debt, collectible through ordinary civil means but no longer backed by the threat of incarceration.

The bill required courts to conduct ability-to-pay hearings before jailing probationers for failure to pay. This codified what *Bearden* had constitutionally required but what many Georgia courts had failed to implement in practice. The requirement shifted responsibility for the indigency determination from private companies—which had a financial interest in finding ability to pay—to courts, which at least theoretically had no such stake. Courts were required to consider evidence of the defendant's financial circumstances, make a specific finding about ability to pay, and consider alternatives before ordering incarceration.

The bill specified factors that courts should consider in determining ability to pay: the defendant's income, whether the defendant was receiving public assistance, the defendant's assets, the defendant's essential expenses, and any other factors affecting the defendant's ability to pay. This provided guidance that had previously been lacking; courts had often made ability-to-pay determinations without clear standards or meaningful inquiry.

The bill allowed courts to convert unpaid fines to community service, providing an alternative for defendants who could not pay. The conversion rate—how many hours of community service would satisfy a given amount of fine—was left to individual courts to determine, but the option was now explicitly authorized by statute. This implemented one of the alternatives that *Bearden* had suggested courts must consider before jailing for nonpayment.

What the bill did not do was equally significant for understanding the system's persistence.

It did not eliminate private probation. Companies could still contract with courts to provide supervision services. The fundamental business model—private companies supervising probationers and charging them fees—remained authorized by law.

It did not cap the fees that companies could charge. A company could still charge $45 per month, or $50, or whatever amount the market would bear and courts would accept. The accumulation of fees that had transformed small fines into large debts could continue, albeit over a shorter period in pay-only cases.

It did not require companies to disclose their revenues, their collection practices, or the total amounts they extracted from probationers. The transparency that advocates had called for—the ability to see how the system actually operated in financial terms—remained limited. Companies continued to treat their financial information as proprietary.

It did not create public alternatives to private probation. For most misdemeanor defendants in most Georgia courts, private supervision remained the only option. A defendant who could not pay a fine immediately would still be assigned to a private company, not to a public probation officer.

Implementation of HB 310 began on July 1, 2015. The legislation represented real reform: some practices that had been documented as abusive were now prohibited by statute. The three-month cap on pay-only probation, in particular, directly addressed one of the industry's most problematic revenue sources. The ability-to-pay hearing requirement gave defendants at least a formal opportunity to demonstrate indigency. But the fundamental structure—private companies supervising probationers and charging them fees for the privilege—remained in place.

## Industry Adaptation: Retreat and Pivot (2015–2020)

The private probation industry's response to HB 310 varied by company. Some companies absorbed the new requirements and continued operating with minimal changes. Others found the regulatory environment more challenging and began to reconsider their presence in Georgia. The most dramatic response came from Sentinel Offender Services, which had been the largest for-profit probation company in the country and one of the most frequently criticized.

In late 2015, just months after HB 310 took effect, Sentinel announced that its Georgia operations were "financially no longer feasible" under the new regulatory environment. The company cited several factors. The three-month cap on pay-only probation, in particular, reduced the revenue that the company could extract from each probationer. Under the old system, a probationer who owed $500 might remain on supervision for two years or longer, paying $40 per month in fees—a total of $960 or more to the company, in addition to whatever payments went toward the underlying fine. Under the new cap, that same probationer would be off supervision after three months if payment was the only condition. The company would receive $120 rather than $960.

The litigation had also imposed costs. Sentinel had been named in multiple lawsuits and had paid significant amounts in settlements and legal fees. The reputational damage had made some courts reluctant to renew contracts. The reform legislation and the litigation together, Sentinel concluded, had made Georgia unprofitable.

Sentinel began selling or abandoning its Georgia contracts, withdrawing from the state over the following year. Some contracts were sold to other companies; others were simply terminated, leaving courts to find replacement providers or to take supervision in-house. By early 2017, Sentinel had exited Georgia entirely. A company spokesperson told the Marshall Project that the "offender-funded" business model had become "politically and fiscally untenable" in the state. Sentinel had supervised probationers in more than 50 Georgia courts; all of those relationships ended.

The lawsuits that had accumulated against Sentinel resulted in settlements totaling more than $2 million. The Walker case, which involved allegations of improper jailing, settled for $200,000. The McGee case, which had resulted in the 2010 injunction, eventually settled for $75,000. The Hucks case settled for $175,000. Other cases settled for smaller amounts. The settlements provided compensation to some of the individuals who had been harmed by Sentinel's practices, but the compensation came only after years of litigation and went only to those who had pursued legal action. The many thousands of probationers who had been subjected to similar practices but had not sued received nothing.

CSRA Probation Services, the Augusta-based company, acquired many of Sentinel's former Georgia contracts. Other companies—including some smaller regional operators—expanded to fill the gap that Sentinel's departure created. The exit of Sentinel did not mean the end of private probation in Georgia; it meant consolidation among the remaining providers. The industry contracted by one major player, but the surviving companies grew to absorb the business that Sentinel had left behind.

Sentinel's corporate trajectory after leaving Georgia illustrates a broader phenomenon: how reform in one area can push private industry into adjacent markets that face fewer regulatory constraints. Unable to profit from probation supervision under the new Georgia regulatory environment, Sentinel pivoted to electronic monitoring—another form of offender-funded criminal justice supervision that the reforms had not directly addressed.

Electronic monitoring involves fitting defendants with GPS ankle bracelets or other devices that track their location and, in some cases, other data such as alcohol consumption. Like probation supervision, electronic monitoring is increasingly "offender-funded": the defendants themselves pay daily or weekly fees for the devices and the monitoring services. And like probation supervision, electronic monitoring raises concerns about the extraction of fees from people who cannot afford them, about the consequences of nonpayment, and about the privatization of governmental functions.

In 2021, Sentinel acquired G4S Justice Services, a division of G4S (now part of Allied Universal), a major international security company that specialized in electronic monitoring and related services. The acquisition gave Sentinel established relationships with courts and corrections departments across the country. In 2022, Sentinel acquired Omnilink Systems, another electronic monitoring provider with proprietary technology and existing contracts. In 2023, Sentinel secured a contract through the National Association of State Procurement Officials (NASPO) to provide electronic monitoring services to agencies in 34 states and territories. The company that had exited Georgia's probation market had repositioned itself in a related market that was larger, growing, and less regulated.

The pattern suggests a kind of hydraulic pressure in criminal justice privatization. When reform closes off one source of revenue—in this case, extended probation supervision fees—companies seek other sources. Electronic monitoring offered Sentinel a business with similar characteristics (offender-funded, recurring fees, governmental contracts) but without the specific reforms that had made probation unprofitable in Georgia. Whether electronic monitoring will eventually face similar scrutiny and reform remains to be seen.

## The Persistence of the System (2018–Present)

In February 2018, Human Rights Watch published a follow-up report: "Set Up to Fail: The Impact of Offender-Funded Private Probation on the Poor." The 142-page investigation documented that, despite the reforms of HB 310, the fundamental problems of private probation persisted in Georgia.

The report was based on interviews with more than 100 people on private probation, as well as defense attorneys, judges, and company employees. It found that private probation companies continued to supervise the majority of misdemeanor probationers in the state. Companies continued to charge monthly fees that many probationers could not afford. The threat of incarceration for nonpayment remained central to the system's operation.

While the three-month cap on pay-only probation had reduced some of the longest extensions, the cap applied only to cases where payment was the sole probation condition. Courts and companies had adapted by ensuring that most probation included at least one additional condition—a requirement to complete an online class, to submit to drug testing, to perform community service, or to attend some form of counseling. Once any condition beyond payment was imposed, the three-month cap did not apply, and probation could extend indefinitely. The reform had created an incentive to impose additional conditions, which increased the fees defendants owed (classes and drug tests cost money) while avoiding the cap.

The report documented cases that echoed those from the pre-reform era. A woman convicted of shoplifting food worth $20 was placed on probation with monthly fees; after eighteen months of payments, she still owed more than she had at the beginning because the fees exceeded what she could pay toward the underlying fine. A man convicted of a traffic offense saw his $200 fine grow to over $1,000 in accumulated fees before he was finally jailed for nonpayment. The stories were familiar, even after reform.

The current scale of private probation in Georgia remains substantial by any measure. Approximately 420,000 Georgians are on some form of probation, giving the state one of the highest probation rates in the country—higher than any other state and several times the national average. More than 80 percent of misdemeanor probationers, by most estimates, are supervised by private companies rather than public probation officers. Approximately 24 companies operate in the state, contracting with courts in nearly every county. The industry remains highly fragmented: no single company dominates the entire state, though several have significant regional presence.

Researchers estimate that these companies extract approximately $40 million per year from probationers through supervision fees, drug testing charges, class fees, and related costs. This figure is necessarily an estimate because companies do not publicly disclose their revenues. The actual amount may be higher. The $40 million represents money transferred from some of the poorest Georgians—people convicted of misdemeanor offenses and unable to pay fines immediately—to private corporations.

CSRA Probation Services has emerged as the dominant provider in eastern Georgia. The company, which acquired many of Sentinel's contracts after Sentinel exited the state, employs more than 150 people and operates 33 offices. It contracts with more than 170 courts, covering approximately one-third of Georgia's counties. The company's website describes it as providing "professional probation services" that help courts "collect court-ordered fines, costs, and fees" while supervising compliance with probation conditions. From the company's perspective, the service is valuable: courts receive the money they are owed, probationers receive supervision that helps them comply with the law, and the company earns a profit for providing these services. From the perspective of defendants who cannot afford the fees, the system operates much as it did before reform—a mechanism for extracting money from people who have none.

The problems documented in earlier investigations continue to appear in local reporting and court records. In December 2022, reporting on the Richmond County jail found that the facility was at 119 percent of capacity, well above safe operating levels. A significant portion of inmates were held on probation violations, many of which involved failure to pay fines and fees. The jail overcrowding created dangerous conditions—inadequate medical care, increased violence, sanitation problems—and cost the county money for overtime, maintenance, and liability. The irony was bitter: private probation had been sold as a way to save counties money by keeping people out of jail, but the system's operation pushed people into jail at substantial public expense.

Federal attention to private probation has increased in recent years. In July 2024, Senator Elizabeth Warren, along with several colleagues, sent letters to ten of the largest private probation companies demanding information about their fee structures, their collection practices, their rates of probation violation, and the total amounts they collect from probationers. The letters cited concerns that the industry was "trapping people in an endless cycle of debt and incarceration" and that the "offender-funded" model created perverse incentives that harmed the people it was supposed to serve.

The letters requested detailed data that companies had historically declined to disclose: how much they collected in fees each year, how many probationers were jailed for nonpayment, how ability-to-pay determinations were made, and what training employees received. The letters noted that previous requests for information from advocacy organizations had been refused and that the lack of transparency made it impossible for the public to evaluate whether the industry operated fairly.

As of early 2026, the companies' responses and any resulting congressional action remain pending. No federal legislation specifically addressing private probation has been enacted or appears imminent. The industry continues to operate largely as it has for three decades, with adjustments for the state-level reforms that have been enacted but no fundamental change in the business model or the underlying dynamics.

## The Current State of Georgia's Private Probation System

Four decades after *Bearden* and three decades after the creation of private probation in Georgia, the system continues to operate. Constitutional doctrine prohibits jailing people solely for inability to pay. State law now requires ability-to-pay hearings before incarceration and caps the length of pay-only probation. Courts have enjoined specific abusive practices and held companies liable for violations.

Yet the fundamental structure remains: private companies supervise hundreds of thousands of Georgians on misdemeanor probation, charge them monthly fees, and use the threat of incarceration to encourage payment. Defendants who cannot afford the fees face consequences that bear no relationship to the seriousness of their original offenses. A traffic ticket can lead to months of supervision and hundreds of dollars in fees. A minor theft can result in extended probation and accumulated debt.

The system has adapted to reform. When HB 310 limited certain revenue streams, some companies exited and others consolidated. When litigation imposed costs, companies settled and adjusted their practices. When political pressure increased, companies defended their business model while pivoting to related markets. The private probation industry has proven resilient.

What has not changed is the underlying dynamic: defendants too poor to pay fines immediately are assigned to private companies that charge them for the privilege of supervision. The fees compound the original debt. The inability to pay leads to consequences. And the cycle continues.

{{< pagebreak >}}

## Timeline: Key Developments in Georgia Private Probation

| Year | Development |
|------|-------------|
| 1970 | *Williams v. Illinois*: cannot extend sentence beyond maximum for inability to pay |
| 1971 | *Tate v. Short*: cannot convert fine-only sentence to jail for indigency |
| 1983 | *Bearden v. Georgia*: cannot revoke probation for inability to pay without willfulness finding |
| 1991 | Georgia legislature authorizes private probation contracts |
| 1992 | First contract: Professional Corrections Services in Chatham County |
| 1992–2005 | Major companies founded: Professional Probation Services, Sentinel, CSRA, JCS |
| 2010 | *McGee v. Sentinel*: federal court enjoins improper arrest warrants |
| 2012 | Private probation operates in 648 Georgia courts |
| 2014 | Human Rights Watch publishes "Profiting from Probation" |
| 2014 | Georgia Supreme Court rules against "tolling" of probation terms |
| 2015 | Governor Deal signs HB 310 (May 7) |
| 2015–2017 | Sentinel exits Georgia; CSRA acquires contracts |
| 2016–2017 | Lawsuit settlements total $2+ million |
| 2018 | Human Rights Watch publishes "Set Up to Fail" |
| 2024 | Senator Warren sends letters to 10 private probation companies |
| Present | ~420,000 Georgians on probation; 80%+ of misdemeanors under private supervision |
